text: The video shows a left-sided ovarian cyst that is completely twisted, indicating torsion.
The surgeon prepares to enter the abdominal cavity using Palmer's point or Misra's point, which are alternative entry sites for laparoscopic access.
The laparoscopic view reveals the entire area of the torted ovarian cyst, showing its size and surrounding inflamed tissue.

The surgeon observes that the left-sided ovarian cyst is adhered to the right side, indicating features of peritonitis. Due to the risk of puncturing the cyst, a supra-umbilical port cannot be placed.

The surgeon introduces the Veress needle, pointing it towards the stomach. After insertion, irrigation, suction, and hanging drop tests are performed. Insufflation is then initiated using preset pressure.
The visual display shows the actual pressure gradually increasing on the monitor during the insufflation process.
The surgeon removes the varus needle after reaching an actual pressure of fifteen and introduces the first optical trocar into the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon points the instrument towards the stomach, preparing to use an adhocastic tube to deflate the stomach for better visualization and access during the procedure.

The surgeon uses a telescope to visualize the surgical field, revealing the ovarian cyst. Under direct vision, another port is placed using the baseball diamond concept, with reference to anatomical landmarks such as the thumb and snuff box for precise positioning.
The surgeon introduces the first optical trocar into the abdominal cavity after reaching the desired pressure.
After placing all the laparoscopic ports, the surgeon prepares to enter the abdominal cavity for the cystectomy procedure.
The surgeon performs an initial diagnostic laparoscopy, visually inspecting the abdominal cavity using a laparoscopic camera.
The laparoscopic view reveals extensive adhesions between the omentum and the right abdominal wall, with the omentum visibly plastered against the wall.
The surgeon uses blunt dissection to detach a gangrenous cyst from the abdominal wall. Despite its appearance as a right-sided ovarian cyst, it is identified as a left-sided cyst.
The surgeon punctures the large cyst, which is too large to be handled with a grasper, allowing for decompression and easier manipulation.
The surgeon uses a suction device to aspirate the contents of the cyst, visibly reducing its size within the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon performs adhesiolysis, gently separating recent adhesions between the omentum, sigmoid colon, and surrounding tissues.

The surgeon performs blunt dissection to separate the unorganized adhesions of the omentum from the ovary, which has started to become gangrenous. The omentum appears to be sealing the ovary, and torsion of the fallopian tube is visible.

The video shows the pedicle of the ovary completely twisted, with the uterus visible nearby. The large ovarian cyst is difficult to untwist, and the fallopian tube is also visible. The surgeon considers using an extracorporeal knot as the best strategy in this situation.
The surgeon ties Mishra's knot using number one Vicryl suture around the pedicle of the ovary. The knot is secured with the aid of a telescope, preparing for the removal of the cyst.
The surgeon uses a grasper to transfer the suture to the same instrument, feeding the suture through four times.
The surgeon demonstrates the application of Mishra's knot with one hitch on the exterior of the surgical field.
The surgeon performs a sequence of knot tying using the Mishra's knot technique, visible as one wind followed by one lock, then a second wind and lock, and finally a third wind.
The surgeon uses a darker knot pusher to tighten the knot securely over the root of the torsion, ensuring stability for the vascular pedicle or tubular structure. The suture is then cut.
The surgeon uses a ligature to separate the ovary, with options to use harmonic or scissors for the separation process.
A grasper is used to apply anteromedial traction, positioning the tissue away from the bowel to prevent accidental injury during the procedure.
The ovary is now visibly separated from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon visually identifies a gangrenous cyst, characterized by darkened and necrotic tissue appearance.
The surgeon prepares to remove the gangrene cyst through the colpotomy wound, following the separation of the ovary.
The surgeon prepares to remove the specimen through the colpotomy route, which is considered the optimal path for extraction in this procedure.
The assistant pushes a sponge, approximately the size of a table tennis ball, through the posterior fornix.
The uterus is lifted, and a sponge is visibly pushed into the posterior fornix between the two uterosacral ligaments.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to address the visible bulge at the colpotomy site, preparing to remove the specimen through the colpotomy wound.
The surgeon cuts the whitish-shiny vaginal fascia, located two centimeters below the arc of the utero-sacral ligament, ensuring the utero-sacral ligament remains undamaged.
The surgeon uses a screwing movement to position a sponge, visible in the posterior fornix between the two utero-sacral ligaments.
The surgeon uses claw forceps positioned beside a sponge that has been inserted through the vagina into the colpotomy wound, visible within the surgical field.

The surgeon observes the bowel adhered to the inflamed cyst, indicating the need for adhesiolysis. Using blunt dissection, the immature adhesion is gently separated, revealing the cyst and surrounding structures.
The claw forceps grasp the cyst, securing it for further manipulation or removal.
The surgeon separates the adhesions between the inflamed cyst and the surrounding tissue using blunt dissection, as previously described.
The surgeon pulls the cyst into the vaginal cavity, where two Cocker forceps are used to hold and bisect the cyst.
The surgeon has completed the adhesiolysis, and all adhesions are now visibly separated from the cyst.
The surgeon uses a speculum to slowly bring the cyst into view and bisect it, draining the remaining fluid from the cyst.

The surgeon uses a valsalam to hold the cervix while a cocker forceps is applied to fix the thin part of the ovary. The cyst is bisected and drained, followed by a zig-zag circular movement to extract the cyst from the surgical field.
The surgeon performs bisection of the cyst and uses suction to drain its contents. The cyst is then visibly extracted from the surgical field.
The cyst is more than fifty percent extracted through the vaginal canal, with care taken to avoid grasping the rectum and surrounding structures.
The surgeon completes the removal of the remaining cyst, which is now fully extracted from the surgical site.
The surgeon has successfully removed the cyst, which is now visible outside the body alongside the ovary. The specimen is prepared to be sent for histopathological examination.
The surgeon performs a thorough lavage of the abdominal cavity, using at least three liters of saline solution while the patient is positioned supine.
The surgeon packs the colpotomy opening with gloves and sponge within the vaginal cavity, followed by thorough lavage to wash out toxic fluid from the surgical area.
The surgeon performs copious lavage to reduce inflammation caused by the presence of toxic Gagarinis fluid in the surgical field.
The surgeon fills the entire abdominal cavity with three liters of saline solution. After the lavage, the vaginal pack is removed, allowing the fluid to drain through the colpotomy wound.
The surgeon shakes the small bubbles, sigmoid colon, and inflamed omentum during the lavage process, ensuring thorough washing of the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon removes the vaginal pack to allow drainage of the saline fluid from the abdominal cavity through the colpotomy wound.
The uterus is visibly covering the colpotomy wound, preventing any herniation of the bowel through the wound.
The surgeon performs repeated abdominal lavage, flushing the cavity with saline until the fluid becomes completely clear, indicating thorough cleansing.
The patient's head is elevated to facilitate the drainage of fluid from Morrison's pouch and other abdominal areas.
The surgeon identifies the gallbladder, liver, left lobe of the liver, and stomach. Fluid is injected throughout the area using a roller multi device under pressure.
The surgeon performs a second wash of the abdominal cavity, ensuring that the fluid becomes clear through repeated irrigation.

A large jar is used to collect clear fluid as the surgeon supports the bobble and removes the vaginal pack. Fluid from the anterior cul-de-sac is suctioned, as it cannot be drained through the vaginal opening.
The surgeon performs multiple lavages using a laparoscopic approach, utilizing 5mm ports and a 10mm telescope positioned at Mishra's point, minimizing visible scarring.
